updates.q: 'Why are you leaving your current role?' -- strongest framing?
updates.a: Pull-not-push: lead with what attracts you to the new role, keep current employer light-positive. Badmouthing signals you will do the same to the next employer in a year.

updates.q: 'Disagree and commit' -- what half do most candidates skip?
updates.a: The COMMIT half. A story where you disagreed AND won is incomplete. Stronger arc: disagreed, brought data, lost, executed fully, validated the team's view in hindsight.

updates.q: Pull-vs-push framing for 'why are you leaving?'
updates.a: Pull: name the specific role/scope/mission attracting you. Push (badmouthing current employer) is the trap -- interviewers read it as a predictor of how you will describe them next year.

Charter still anchors on AGI that benefits humanity, now operationalized into five principles: Democratization, Empowerment, Universal Prosperity, Resilience, Adaptability. Culture rewards intensity, scale, and shipping products users love. Bring a role-specific reason, not a mission quote.

Show real engagement with safety: RSP v3.0 (Feb 2026 -- replaced hard pauses with a public Frontier Safety Roadmap plus recurring risk assessments), Constitutional AI, and the Acceptable Use Policy. Generic 'I do the right thing' answers fail ethical-dilemma probes.

new_cards.q: In STAR, what fraction of a strong answer should be Action?
new_cards.a: Roughly 60%. Situation and Task together are ~20% (2-3 sentences of context, plus a one-line scope of YOUR ownership), Action is ~60% (specific technical and interpersonal moves you personally made), Result is ~20% (technical outcome + quantified business impact).

new_cards.q: Why is 'we' a red flag in behavioral answers, and how do you fix it mid-story?
new_cards.a: 'We' hides individual ownership -- the exact thing FDE rounds probe. Fix: switch to 'I' for every decision and action ('I escalated to the on-call', 'I wrote the rollback script'), and reserve 'we' only for true group context.

new_cards.q: What three elements separate a 'real failure' story from a sanitized one at Palantir?
new_cards.a: (1) A concrete mechanism -- what actually broke and why. (2) Real cost -- dollars, users, downtime, or trust lost. (3) A systemic change you made afterward (process, monitoring, or design) so it cannot recur. Without all three it reads as a humblebrag.

new_cards.q: Three instant-reject failure-story openers at Palantir?
new_cards.a: (1) 'I work too hard.' (2) 'I'm a perfectionist.' (3) 'I don't really fail / I can't think of one.' All signal lack of self-awareness or unwillingness to own a real mistake.

new_cards.q: What single detail most strengthens a 'production fix under pressure' story?
new_cards.a: A timestamped sequence with the specific decision points -- e.g., 'at T+4min I rolled back the deploy, at T+11min I patched the config, at T+23min traffic recovered'. Specificity proves you actually owned the incident rather than narrating someone else's.

new_cards.q: When pushing back on a customer request, what must the Action section include?
new_cards.a: The alternative you proposed and why it served the customer's underlying goal better -- not just 'I said no'. Strong stories show you preserved the relationship by reframing the ask, often with a smaller scoped pilot or a different mechanism that hit the same outcome.

new_cards.q: In a deployment-failure story, what distinguishes 'ownership' from 'involvement'?
new_cards.a: Ownership means you ran the rollback, the comms, AND the postmortem -- and the systemic fix shipped under your name. Involvement is 'I helped the on-call'. Interviewers listen for first-person verbs on every step of the recovery.

new_cards.q: How do you explain a hard technical limit to a non-technical stakeholder without sounding dismissive?
new_cards.a: Translate the constraint into their unit of value (revenue, latency for their users, regulatory risk), offer 2-3 ranked alternatives with tradeoffs, and let them choose. Never end with 'it's impossible' -- end with 'here are the options and what each costs'.

new_cards.q: What makes a 'decision under incomplete information' story strong vs weak?
new_cards.a: Strong: you name the specific unknowns, the cheapest experiment you ran to reduce them, your decision criteria, and what you would have done differently with hindsight. Weak: 'I went with my gut and it worked out' -- gives no signal on judgment.

new_cards.q: Why is 3-5 deep stories better prep than 20 shallow ones?
new_cards.a: Each deep story can be retold from multiple angles (failure, conflict, leadership, customer-first) to answer ~80% of behavioral prompts. Twenty shallow stories crumble under follow-up probing because you cannot recall mechanism, metric, or sequence.

new_cards.q: What are the four specificity hooks that make a behavioral answer feel real?
new_cards.a: (1) Named people or teams (anonymized if needed). (2) Dates or durations ('over six weeks', 'in Q3 2024'). (3) Quantified metrics (dollars, percent, users, latency). (4) The exact tool/system named ('Postgres replica', 'Terraform module').

new_cards.q: Customer-first vs engineering-pride -- how do you signal the right tradeoff?
new_cards.a: Tell a story where you shipped a less elegant solution because the customer needed it Friday, and you scheduled the refactor as a follow-up. The signal is that you optimized for customer outcome on the critical path, not for your own technical aesthetics.

new_cards.q: What is the strongest pattern for 'leading without authority'?
new_cards.a: Identify a cross-team gap nobody owned, write the doc/spec, get 2-3 key stakeholders to commit in 1:1s before any group meeting, then run the meeting against your draft. The Action is influence work (pre-wiring, written artifacts), not formal authority.

new_cards.q: How should you recover mid-interview after giving a weak behavioral answer?
new_cards.a: Name it: 'That answer rambled -- let me give you the 30-second version.' Then deliver tight STAR. Self-awareness plus correction is itself a positive signal; pretending it went well is worse than acknowledging.

new_cards.q: How do you tailor the same story for OpenAI vs Anthropic vs Palantir?
new_cards.a: Same arc, different emphasis: OpenAI -- shipping velocity and user impact at scale. Anthropic -- the safety/risk reasoning and tradeoffs you weighed. Palantir -- ownership, on-the-ground customer work, and explicit failure-mechanism honesty.

new_cards.q: Two Palantir-specific 'why us' traps to avoid?
new_cards.a: (1) Confusing Foundry (commercial data platform) with Gotham (government/intel) -- mixing them up signals you did not do basic research. (2) Citing comp, perks, or 'cool problems' instead of a specific product, customer segment, or mission angle you can defend with detail.
